messages: Also, the only person who can legally sign off my supervised time is my parent, but they only have time sporadically and are uncomfortable supervising any fast-road runs.
Given that, what are realistic ways to get some controlled higher-speed experience without risking safety or wasting the limited sign-off time?
A final piece of context about the car: it's older and the engagement between engine and gearbox is quite sensitive - if I mishandle things it jerks and sounds terrible, which I want to avoid.
Any tips on practicing precise engagement and hill starts that won't quickly wear the car or make every practice feel like a repair job?
Okay - with all of that in mind, could you put together a practical, realistic training plan for me?
Preferably week-by-week for the period I booked, with progressive skill targets so I can see what to work on each session.
For each session suggest a concise warm-up, two or three targeted drills, and suggested durations that fit someone who only has a few weekday evenings and a couple of longer practice blocks.
Please make sure the plan gradually introduces faster joining maneuvers and confined urban parking so I'm ready for that local test mix.
I also need the schedule to ensure I accumulate the required logged driving time - recommend how to spread those hours across different conditions so they count and build competence.
Because the car's gear engagement is sensitive, include specific low-risk drills to develop a delicate touch when changing gears and starting on slopes.
Since the only eligible log signatory has limited availability and avoids fast-road sessions, propose safe alternatives for getting higher-speed exposure that still count.
Add a short list of anxiety-management routines to use before and during drives so I don't get overwhelmed during a fast approach.
Also provide a printable weekly log template with headings and one example entry formatted like an accepted log entry.
Please include simple maintenance tips to protect the older family vehicle while I'm practicing fine control - things to watch for and small ways to avoid premature wear.
Give me 8-10 honest 'I can...' milestones to use as gate checks before scheduling a mock test.
Finally, when you deliver that plan, focus on steady, staged progress across the whole period I booked and prioritize safe, confidence-building practices over trying to rush through the hours.
